coredata.dc:title: Small-sized molybdenum disulfide nanocomposites for multifunctional responsive water treatment based on carbon point modulation: Effective adsorption of rhodamine B and malachite green as well as hexavalent chromium and bacteria
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qing
Zhang, Bin
Hu, Yanling
Yang, Zhengfang
Guo, Qianqian
Wen, Guanbin
Wang, Qisheng
Nie, Yanli
Wang, Dongquan
coredata.dc:description: Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) possesses a unique layered structure, high specific surface area, abundant unsaturated sites, and weak interlayer van der Waals forces, which make it suitable for widespread use in environmental protection. However, its hydrophobicity and relatively low dispersibility bring forward several challenges. It can easily agglomerate and accumulate in water, which seriously affects its application in the water pollution treatment process. Carbon dots (CDs) offer the advantages of high water solubility, good dispersibility, excellent photostability, negligible cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility. In this study, multifunctional responsive adsorbent materials for treatment of polluted water were prepared by combining CDs with MoS2. Appearance and morphology, structural composition, pore size and specific surface area, adsorption performance, antimicrobial performance, antimicrobial mechanism, and bacterial damage of these nanocomposites were also evaluated. Further, the adsorption performance data of the composite were analyzed by using adsorption model fitting. The small-sized CDs-MoS2 composites based on carbon point modulation effectively adsorbed the cationic dye rhodamine B, anionic dye malachite green, as well as the heavy metal ion hexavalent chromium and bacterial microorganisms. This material shows potential application prospects for multifunctional response to polluted water treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs-MoS2
Adsorption
Antibacterial
RhB
MG
Cr6+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Adsorption properties of CDs-MoS2 toward cationic dye RhB
Adsorption properties of CDs-MoS2 toward anionic dye MG
Adsorption properties of CDs-MoS2 toward heavy metal ion Cr6+
Study of antimicrobial properties of CDs-MoS2
Analysis of antimicrobial activity of CDs-MoS2
Analysis of the optimal amount of CDs for the preparation of CDs-MoS2
Analysis of optimal antimicrobial concentration of CDs-MoS2
Antimicrobial treatment of actual water bodies

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM (500 nm); (b) SEM (200 nm); (c) TEM (10 nm); (d)–(i) TEM (100 nm) element mapping images of CDs-MoS2.

(a) XRD pattern of CDs-MoS2, (b) FTIR spectra of CDs, MoS2, and CDs-MoS2, (c) XPS full spectrum, (d) high-resolution XPS pattern of C1s, (e) high-resolution XPS pattern of Mo3d, and (f) high-resolution XPS pattern of S2p.
N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms: (a) MoS2; (c) CDs-MoS2. Pore size distribution: (b) MoS2; (d) CDs-MoS2.
UV absorption curves of RhB in the presence of (a) CDs, (b) MoS2, (c) CDs-MoS2, and (d) decolorization efficiency.
UV absorption curves of MG in the presence of (a) CDs, (b) MoS2, (c) CDs-MoS2, and (d) decolorization efficiency.
UV absorption curves of Cr6+ in the presence of (a) CDs, (b) MoS2, and (c) CDs-MoS2; and (d) adsorption efficiency.
Pseudo-first-order kinetic model: (a) RhB, (c) MG, (e) Cr6+. Pseudo-second-order kinetic model: (b) RhB, (d) MG, and (f) Cr6+.

Langmuir adsorption isotherm model: (a) RhB, (e) MG, (i) Cr6+. Freundlich adsorption isotherm model: (b) RhB, (f) MG, (j) Cr6+. Dubinin–Radushkevich (D–R) linear fitted adsorption isotherm model: (c) RhB, (g) MG, (k) Cr6+. D–R nonlinear fitted adsorption isotherm model: (d) RhB, (h) MG, (l) Cr6+.
Intraparticle diffusion modeling of (a) RhB, (b) MG, and (c) Cr6+.
(a) Photographs of antimicrobial experiments of MoS2 and CDs-MoS2 and (b) histogram of rate of antimicrobial activity.
Optimal dosage of CDs for the preparation of CDs-MoS2: (a) antibacterial experimental plots and (b) antibacterial rate histogram. (A: 20 mL CDs; B: 40 mL CDs; C: 60 mL CDs; D: 80 mL CDs).

Optimal antimicrobial dosage of CDs-MoS2: (a) photograph of antimicrobial experiments, (b) histogram of antimicrobial rate. (A: 0.0001 g·mL−1; B: 0.0002 g·mL−1; C: 0.0003 g·mL−1; D: 0.0004 g·mL−1; E: 0.0005 g·mL−1).

The antimicrobial mechanism of CDs-MoS2: (a) photograph of antimicrobial experiments, (b) histogram of antimicrobial rate, (c) pie chart of antimicrobial mechanism of E. coli, and (d) pie chart of antimicrobial mechanism of S. aureus.
(a) Analysis of the metabolic activity of bacterial cells and activity of the respiratory chain dehydrogenase of bacteria and (b) analysis of the degree of lipid peroxidation of bacteria.
(a) Bacterial morphology of E. coli and (b) CDs-MoS2 in contact with E. coli; bacterial morphology of S. aureus and (d) CDs-MoS2 in contact with S. aureus.

(a) Antibacterial effect of CDs-MoS2 on bacteria in Dianchi water samples; (b) Pie chart of the number of viable bacteria in Dianchi water samples; and (c) Pie chart of the number of dead bacteria in Dianchi water samples after treatment with CDs-MoS2.

head.ce:title: Small-sized molybdenum disulfide nanocomposites for multifunctional responsive water treatment based on carbon point modulation: Effective adsorption of rhodamine B and malachite green as well as hexavalent chromium and bacteria

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) possesses a unique layered structure, high specific surface area, abundant unsaturated sites, and weak interlayer van der Waals forces, which make it suitable for widespread use in environmental protection. However, its hydrophobicity and relatively low dispersibility bring forward several challenges. It can easily agglomerate and accumulate in water, which seriously affects its application in the water pollution treatment process. Carbon dots (CDs) offer the advantages of high water solubility, good dispersibility, excellent photostability, negligible cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility. In this study, multifunctional responsive adsorbent materials for treatment of polluted water were prepared by combining CDs with MoS2. Appearance and morphology, structural composition, pore size and specific surface area, adsorption performance, antimicrobial performance, antimicrobial mechanism, and bacterial damage of these nanocomposites were also evaluated. Further, the adsorption performance data of the composite were analyzed by using adsorption model fitting. The small-sized CDs-MoS2 composites based on carbon point modulation effectively adsorbed the cationic dye rhodamine B, anionic dye malachite green, as well as the heavy metal ion hexavalent chromium and bacterial microorganisms. This material shows potential application prospects for multifunctional response to polluted water treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the changing environment, the prevalence of bacteria in nature is increasing dramatically, which seriously affects human health and poses severe threat to human life and environment. Therefore, appropriate measures must be taken to address these issues [1]. Industrial wastewater contains numerous toxic organic pollutants as well as toxic heavy metal ions and bacterial microorganisms. Therefore, it is essential to design multifunctional responsive materials capable of not only eliminating various pollutants associated with water, but also killing bacterial microorganisms in water to meet the water purification needs [2,3]. Organic dyes with complex chemical structures, are resistant to degradation and usually contain toxic compounds requiring challenging and expensive treatment. Heavy metal ions are ubiquitous and persistent in the environment, with severe toxicity and risk of bioaccumulation. Pathogenic bacteria in water sources pose a major threat to the environment and human health, and water bodies have thus become vectors for disease transmission. Together, these contaminants highlight the urgent need for efficient water treatment solutions to protect ecosystems and public health [4].

Various pollutants require different treatment methods, leading to higher costs and lower efficiency. Therefore, development of water treatment materials that can simultaneously remove multiple pollutants is crucial and demands immediate systematic explorations. Common methods for the removal of organic dyes and heavy metal ions include chemical reduction precipitation, membrane separation, photolysis, high-temperature pyrolysis, ion exchange, and adsorption. Among these methods, adsorption stands out due to its methodological simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and minimal secondary contamination, making it an effective method for pollutant removal [5]. In recent years, adsorbent materials such as cellulose, biochar, zeolite, montmorillonite, etc., have been extensively used [6,7], but their associated drawbacks, such as low adsorption capacity and poor selectivity, limit their further development [8]. Therefore, it is crucial to design multifunctional adsorbents with antimicrobial properties combined with high adsorption properties [9].

As a typical two-dimensional (2D) material, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) consists of a layered structure with triatomic layers (S–Mo–S), which are held together by weak van der Waals interactions [10]. Despite these weak interactions, MoS2 exhibits excellent physicochemical properties, including remarkable chemical stability and structural designability, due to its specialized layered structure [11–13]. Many researchers have prepared different MoS2-based nanomaterials through various methods such as thermal decomposition of precursors, electrochemical deposition, and hydrothermal methods [14]. It has been demonstrated that flaky inorganic compounds with intrinsic layered structure may be suitable for environmental pollution control [15]. MoS2, as an inexpensive and representative inorganic material, is similar to graphene layered structure. It has a high specific surface area, abundant unsaturated sites, unique layered structure, and weak interlayer van der Waals forces, which make it a potential candidate for environmental protection [16]. However, it is a hydrophobic substance with relatively low dispersibility, and it easily undergoes agglomeration and accumulation in water, which seriously affects its application in the treatment process of polluted water. This can prevent numerous active sites of MoS2 from effectively contacting the pollutants in the water body, thus adversely affecting the removal efficiency toward these pollutants [17]. To overcome the disadvantage of its poor dispersion, surface modification is an effective strategy to increase the efficacy of active sites on MoS2.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new class of carbon nanomaterials with dimensions less than 10 nm, were first obtained in 2004 during the preparation of purified single-walled carbon nanotubes by electrophoresis [18]. Further, in 2006, Sun et al. named this new class of materials [19]. In recent years, CDs have attracted significant attention due to their numerous excellent properties, including high water solubility, good dispersibility, excellent photostability, negligible cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, easy surface modification, and excellent chemical inertness [20]. They have been widely used in biological fields such as biosensing [21,22], cell imaging [23,24], drug delivery [25,26], antimicrobial [27,28] and anticancer, and tumor therapy [29–35]. Natural biowastes and their derived carbon materials have received widespread attention for their cost-effectiveness, renewability, and environment-friendliness [5]. In this study, CDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method using hard macadamia nut shells from Lincang, Yunnan Province.

Biochar obtained from the thermal conversion of biomass waste has been shown to be a suitable matrix for fabricating metal–biochar composites as an effective material for removing oxygenated anionic pollutants from wastewater [36]. Therefore, in this study, CDs with high water solubility and good dispersibility were composited with MoS2, which could not only improve the dispersibility of MoS2 and increase its effective active sites, but also facilitate the recycling of waste biomass resources and promote the multifunctional response of biochar composite adsorbents during the water treatment process. Further, adsorption experiments were conducted on cationic dye rhodamine B (RhB), anionic dye malachite green (MG), as well as heavy metal ions hexavalent chromium (Cr6+) and bacterial microorganisms to evaluate the adsorbing capability of the synthesized composite. Based on the fact that most of the previous studies on adsorbents exhibited only a single-function response, the present study reveals a multifunctional response, which provides a certain theoretical basis and experimental foundation for multifunctional-response materials for treatment of polluted water bodies.

The shells of macadamia nuts were washed and dried at 100 °C for 12 h, and then crushed. A certain amount of brown macadamia nut shell powder was weighed and dispersed in a small beaker containing distilled water, and the contents were stirred to make the dispersion homogeneous. Next, the dispersion was put into an ultrasonic cleaner and ultrasonicated at room temperature for 10 min. The ultrasonicated dispersion was transferred into a high-pressure reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene, and then subjected to a hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 14 h, and then naturally cooled down to room temperature. The sample solution was obtained by filtering large particles and insoluble impurities through 0.1-µm aqueous microporous filter membrane, and the solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

To an appropriate amount of deionized water, sodium molybdate and thiourea were added. The resulting mixture was stirred at room temperature for 1 h, transferred to an autoclave at 200 °C and kept under this condition for 12 h. After completion of the reaction, the resulting lab-made MoS2 was collected after cooling and washing with ethanol.

To an appropriate amount of CDs solution, sodium molybdate and thiourea were added. The obtained mixture was stirred at room temperature for 1 h, transferred to an autoclave at 200 °C and kept under this condition for 12 h. After completion of the reaction, the resulting CDs-MoS2 nanocomposites were collected after cooling and washing with ethanol.

The adsorption properties of CDs-MoS2 nanocomposites were evaluated using the pollutant models, cationic dye RhB and anionic dye MG, as well as heavy metal ion Cr6+. The suspensions were stirred continuously at regular intervals, next they were taken and the insoluble material was filtered off using a pinhole filter membrane, and subjected to ultraviolet (UV) spectrophotometry in the spectral scanning range of 400–800 nm. The adsorption efficiency of CDs-MoS2 nanocomposites for RhB was determined by monitoring the change in maximum absorbance at 554 nm. The adsorption efficiency of CDs-MoS2 nanocomposites for MG was determined by monitoring the change in maximum absorbance at 618 nm, and that of CDs-MoS2 nanocomposites for Cr6+ was determined by monitoring the change in maximum absorbance at 540 nm.

The Gram-negative bacterium Escherichia coli (E. coli, CCTCC204033) and Gram-positive bacterium Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC25723) were selected as the experimental strains for antibacterial detection. First, Luria–Bertani (LB) agar medium (tryptone 5 g, yeast powder 2.5 g, sodium chloride 5 g, distilled water 500 mL, agar 7.5 g) was configured, and the plate was inverted and set aside. Then, LB liquid medium (tryptone 5 g, yeast powder 2.5 g, sodium chloride 5 g, distilled water 500 mL) was configured, and the bacterial strains were added to the appropriate amount of sterile liquid medium. The bacterial suspension was made by incubation, and diluted to the appropriate level of sterile water. The bacterial suspension was diluted to 1 × 105 CFU·mL−1 with sterile water. CDs-MoS2 nanocomposites were mixed with the diluted bacterial solution in sterile test tubes to formulate the suspension. All test tubes were incubated at 37 °C, 200 rpm on a shaking bed for 15 min to attain uniformity and then removed. Subsequently, 100-µL drops of the upper layer of liquid were aspirated into a Petri dish, and the bacterial solution was evenly coated on the culture medium by the coating plate method. The petri dishes were placed in a constant-temperature incubator for 18–24 h. After incubation, the antimicrobial rate of each sample was calculated by colony counting method. The calculation formula is as follows:
(1)
%
( Antibacterial rate )
=
N
Control
−
N
Sample
N
Control
✕ %
where NControl is the number of colonies in the blank control group and NSample denotes the number of colonies after the addition of sample-coated plate.

The crystalline structure of CDs-MoS2 was analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Ultima IV, Japan). The microstructures of CDs and CDs-MoS2 were analyzed by field-emission transmission electron microscopy (FETEM, JEOL JEM-2100F, Japan). The surface functional groups of CDs-MoS2 were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific iN10). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA) was used to analyze the molecular composition of CDs-MoS2. Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, NOVANANOSEM 450) was employed to characterize the microscopic morphology of CDs-MoS2 and the bacterial morphology after contact with bacteria. Further, the specific surface area and pore size distribution of CDs-MoS2 were characterized using a fully-automated specific surface and porosity analyzer (BET, Micromeritics ASAP 2460).

Fig. S1 shows the TEM images and size distribution images of CDs, which show regular spherical particles. Moreover, the particle size distribution map shows that the particles are spherical with an average diameter of about 4.25 nm, with their particle sizes ranging from 1–9 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images show that the CDs exhibit a good lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [37].

The microscopic morphology of CDs-MoS2 was observed by SEM and TEM, as shown in Fig. 1. Fig. 1(a), (b) show the SEM images of CDs-MoS2. Fig. 1(c)–(i) exhibit the TEM images of CDs-MoS2 and the EDS elemental mapping images. Fig. S2 shows the SEM image of MoS2, clearly exhibiting that the size of the CDs-MoS2 composite decreases after modulation with CDs. Fig. 1(a), (b) illustrate that the CDs-MoS2 composites exhibit a micro-morphology consisting of nano-sized cotton-like porous structure. The assembly of CDs on the smooth MoS2 surface is shown in Fig. 1(c), revealing the successful formation of the composite material. Further Fig. 1(d)–(i) show the EDS elemental mapping images, exhibiting the uniform distribution of the C, O, S, and Mo elements in the CDs-MoS2 composites. Further, Fig. S3 shows the EDS energy spectral analysis of the CDs-MoS2 nanocomposites, revealing that the content of C is 30.20 %; the content of O is 7.05 %; the content of S is 28.03 %; and that of Mo is 34.72 %. Consequently, this result indicates the successful preparation of the composites.

For examining the structure and composition of CDs-MoS2 and to analyze its crystalline phase, XRD analysis was carried out. Fig. 2(a) shows the XRD patterns of MoS2 and CDs-MoS2. The intensity of peaks for MoS2 in CDs-MoS2 decreases, which may be attributed to the fact that the introduction of CDs changes the degree of crystallinity, which transforms the ordered structure to the disordered structure. It may also be ascribed to the smaller particle size of CDs-MoS2. That is, the smaller the particles, the lower the intensity of the diffraction peaks, which is consistent with the results of the adsorption characterization analysis revealing the increase in the specific surface area. The FTIR spectra were used to characterize the surface functional groups (Fig. 2b). The FTIR spectrum of CDs shows a blunt peak at 3312.4 cm−1, which is attributed to the O–H stretching vibration, and the vibration peak at 1635.9 cm−1 is attributed to CO [38]. It indicates that the CDs consist of hydrophilic groups and are well dispersed. The enhanced intensity of the peaks at the corresponding positions of the CDs-MoS2 composites indicates the successful fabrication of the composites. Moreover, the peak positions were red-shifted or blue-shifted to some extent. This may be due to the existence of certain interactions between CDs and MoS2. The molecular composition of CDs-MoS2 was further determined by XPS. The full-range XPS spectra (Fig. 2c) show the presence of four typical elements, C, O, S, and Mo, in CDs-MoS2, and the percentages of C, O, S, and Mo are 58.82 %, 24.37 %, 9.99 %, and 6.83 %, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. 2d) show three peaks at 284.8, 285.7, and 288.7 eV, which are respectively attributed to C–C/CC [37], C–O–C/S [39], and CO. Among them, the peak area of C–O–C/S is the largest, indicating that mainly C–O–C/S is dominant. The Mo3d high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 2e) shows five peaks at 226.4, 228.9, 229.2, 232.7, and 236 eV, which are attributed to S2s [38], Mo4+3d5/2, Mo4+3d3/2, Mo6+3d5/2, and Mo6+ 3d3/2, respectively. Among them, the peak area of Mo6+3d5/2 is the largest, indicating that it is mainly dominated by Mo6+. High-resolution S2p XPS spectra (Fig. 2f) exhibit four peaks at 161.8, 163.1, 164, and 168.8 eV, attributed to S2−2p3/2, S2−2p1/2, S–C, and S4+, respectively [40]. Among them, the peak area of S2−2p3/2 is the largest, indicating that it is mainly dominated by S2−. High-resolution C1s XPS spectra and high-resolution S2p XPS spectra proved the formation of C–O–C/S, S–C. It further illustrates the successful preparation of CDs-MoS2 composites.

To investigate the pore structure and specific surface area of the materials, the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore size distribution of MoS2 and CDs-MoS2 composites were investigated. Fig. 3(a) demonstrates that the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of MoS2 were not closed, probably due to the small specific surface area. The nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of CDs-MoS2 showed a type IV isotherm hysteresis closure (Fig. 3c), which confirms the mesoporous structure enclosed by pores in the form of narrow slits [41]. Compared with MoS2 (Fig. 3b), the pore size distribution of CDs-MoS2 (Fig. 3d) composites moved toward the small pore size direction. Table S1 presents that the average pore sizes of MoS2 and CDs-MoS2 composites were 25.3532 and 16.3795 nm, respectively. The specific surface areas of MoS2 and CDs-MoS2 composites were 0.6342 and 3.2855 m2·g−1, respectively. The results indicate that the increase in the specific surface area and the decrease in pore sizes of CDs-MoS2 composites favor the enhancement of its adsorption performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption properties of CDs-MoS2 toward cationic dye RhB
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption performance of 10 mg of CDs-MoS2 toward 10 mg·L−1 of the cationic dye RhB was investigated. Fig. 4(c) shows the adsorption degradation activity corresponding to the UV absorption spectra of the cationic dye RhB. The maximum wavelength of the UV absorption peak of RhB was 554 nm. Next, the CDs-MoS2 composite was added into the RhB solution with continuous stirring, and the solution was taken at certain intervals for full-spectrum UV absorption spectroscopy analysis. After 30 min, about 70.6 % of RhB was adsorbed. By contrast, only about 23.6 % of RhB was adsorbed when MoS2 alone was added to the RhB solution. It indicates that the CDs-MoS2 composite shows a good adsorption effect on RhB. After 120 min, the adsorption reaches the equilibrium, the adsorption and decolorization efficiency of CDs-MoS2 on RhB reaches more than 95 %, whereas MoS2 alone shows an adsorption and decolorization efficiency of about 37 %. Therefore, it can be concluded that the CDs-MoS2 composite shows better adsorption degradation effect on RhB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption properties of CDs-MoS2 toward anionic dye MG
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption performance of 10 mg of CDs-MoS2 toward 10 mg·L−1 of the anionic dye MG was investigated. Fig. 5(c) shows the UV absorption spectra showing the adsorption degradation activity of CDs-MoS2 on the anionic dye MG. The maximum wavelength of the UV absorption peak of MG was 618 nm. The CDs-MoS2 composite was added to the MG solution with continuous stirring, and the solution was taken at certain intervals for conducting the full-spectrum UV absorption spectroscopy analysis. After 10 min, about 74 % of MG was adsorbed on CDs-MoS2, while only 45 % of MG was adsorbed on MoS2. After 30 min, the adsorption reached equilibrium, and the adsorption and decolorization efficiency of CDs-MoS2 toward MG reached more than 98 %, while only 63 % of MG was adsorbed on MoS2. It indicates that the CDs-MoS2 composite also shows excellent adsorption effect toward MG. Compared with that toward the cationic dye RhB, the CDs-MoS2 composite material shows a better adsorption effect toward MG, which can be almost completely adsorbed in just 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption properties of CDs-MoS2 toward heavy metal ion Cr6+
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption performance of 10 mg of CDs-MoS2 toward 1000 µg·L−1 of heavy metal ion Cr6+ was further explored. Fig. 6(c) shows the UV absorption spectra corresponding to the adsorption degradation activity of CDs-MoS2 toward heavy metal ion Cr6+. The maximum wavelength of the UV absorption peak of Cr6+ after color development by the chromogenic agent was 540 nm. Next, the CDs-MoS2 composite was added to the Cr6+ solution with continuous stirring, and the solution was taken at certain intervals for investigating the UV absorption full spectrum. After 15 min, about 42 % of Cr6+ was adsorbed by CDs-MoS2. When MoS2 alone was added to the Cr6+ solution, only about 5 % of Cr6+ was adsorbed by MoS2. However, after 90 min, the adsorption reached equilibrium. CDs-MoS2 achieved 64 % adsorption efficiency toward Cr6+, while only about 16 % adsorption efficiency was achieved by MoS2 alone. It indicates that the CDs-MoS2 composite shows a better adsorption effect on Cr6+.

The fit of the resulting data was evaluated using a pseudo-first-order dynamics model and a pseudo-second-order dynamics model ( Fig. 7).
(2)
Pseudo − first − order kinetic model equations : log
q
e
−
q
t
= log
q

−
k
1
2.303
t
(3)
Pseudo second − order kinetic model equations :
t
q
t
=
t
q
e
+
1
k
2
q
e
2

where qt (mg·g−1) is the adsorption capacity at time t; t (min) is the adsorption time; qe (mg·g−1) is the equilibrium adsorption capacity; k1 denotes the rate constant for the pseudo-first-order kinetic model; and k2 is the rate constant for the pseudo-second-order kinetic model.

The fitting results indicate that the pseudo-first-order kinetic adsorption model was not applicable to the adsorption process and the pseudo-second-order kinetic adsorption model was suitable to explain the adsorption process. Table 1 presents that the pseudo-first-order kinetic adsorption model R2 for RhB is 0.9878, the pseudo-first-order kinetic adsorption model R2 for MG is 0.9991, and the pseudo-first-order kinetic adsorption model R2 for Cr6+ is 0.9813. The pseudo- second -order kinetic adsorption model R2 for RhB is 0.9969, the pseudo- second -order kinetic adsorption model R2 for MG is 0.9992, and the pseudo- second -order kinetic adsorption model R2 for Cr6+ is 0.9972. According to the pseudo-second-order kinetic adsorption model, R2 is larger than the pseudo-first-order kinetic adsorption model R2, thus the adsorption process of CDs-MoS2 composites toward RhB, MG, and Cr6+conforms to the pseudo-second-order kinetic adsorption model.

Adsorption isotherms are used to understand how adsorbed molecules are distributed between the aqueous and solid phases under equilibrium conditions [42,43].
(4)
Langmuir model equations :

=
k
1

m

/ ( 1 +

(5)

2

1 / n
(6)
Dubinin – Radushkevich ( D – R ) model equations : In
q

= In
q
m

k
3
ε
2

where qe (mg·g−1) is the equilibrium adsorption capacity; ce (mg·L−1) is the concentration of the pollutant in solution at the adsorption equilibrium; qm (mg·g−1) denotes the maximum adsorption capacity; ε is the Polanyi potential; k1 is the Langmuir’s constant; k2 denotes the Freundlich constant; and k3 is the D–R constant.

The experimental data were fitted and analyzed by using the abovementioned three adsorption isotherm models ( Fig. 8). The results presented in Table 2 show that the Langmuir model R2 for RhB was 0.96982; the Langmuir model R2 for MG was 0.99584; and the Langmuir model R2 for Cr6+ was 0.3984. The Freundlich model R2 for RhB was 0.79426; the Freundlich model R2 for MG was 0.88321; and the Freundlich model R2 for Cr6+ was 0.84949. The (D–R) linear fitting model R2 for RhB was 0.97595; the (D–R) linear fitting model R2 for MG was 0.99391; and the (D–R) linear fitting model R2 for Cr6+ was 0.87138. The RhB (D–R) nonlinear fitting model R2 was 0.9754; for MG, (D–R) nonlinear fitting model R2 was 0.99389; and for Cr6+, (D–R) nonlinear fitting model R2 was 0.9252. For RhB, (D–R) linear fitting model R2 > (D–R) nonlinear fitting model R2 > Langmuir model R2 > Freundlich model R2. This result indicates that the adsorption process of CDs-MoS2 composites on RhB conforms to the (D–R) linear fitting adsorption isotherm model. For MG, Langmuir model R2 > (D–R) linear fitting model R2 > (D–R) nonlinear fitting model R2 > Freundlich model R2. It suggests that the adsorption process of CDs-MoS2 composites on MG conforms to Langmuir adsorption isotherm model. For Cr6+, (D–R) nonlinear fitting model R2 > (D–R) linear fitting model R2 > Freundlich model R2 > Langmuir model R2. This result suggests that the adsorption process of CDs-MoS2 composites for Cr6+ conforms to the (D–R) nonlinear fitting adsorption isotherm model.

To study the mechanism of the adsorption process and to determine the factors controlling the adsorption–diffusion, the adsorption data of CDs-MoS2 composites for different pollutants were analyzed by using the intraparticle diffusion model ( Fig. 9).
(7)
Model equations for intraparticle diffusion :
q
t
= k
t
1 / 2
+ C
where qt (mg·g−1) is the adsorbed amount at time t; t (min) is the adsorption time; k is the intraparticle diffusion rate constant; and C denotes the modeling constant.

Fig. 9 presents the analysis of the data obtained by fitting the intraparticle diffusion model, revealing that the adsorption of all three different pollutants by the CDs-MoS2 composites can be divided into three phases. The slope of the first phase is the largest among the three phases with high adsorption rate. This phase corresponds to surface diffusion, which is mainly controlled by membrane diffusion. In this phase, the solution concentration is high and the mass transfer driving force is also high, which makes the adsorption rate faster and the K value is larger. The second stage corresponds to mesopore diffusion, where the adsorption sites of CDs-MoS2 composites are gradually saturated, the solution concentration gradually decreases, and the internal diffusion resistance increases. The adsorption process in this stage is controlled by membrane diffusion and intraparticle diffusion. The third stage corresponds to the intra-micropore diffusion, which is the final equilibrium stage, where the adsorption sites are saturated and the adsorption and desorption rates are basically the same. The fitted lines for the first two stages do not cross the origin, indicating that the diffusion step is controlled by several factors, such as membrane diffusion and internal diffusion [42,44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of antimicrobial properties of CDs-MoS2
Analysis of antimicrobial activity of CDs-MoS2
ce:section.ce:para: The antimicrobial activity of MoS2 and the composite CDs-MoS2 was investigated using E. coli (CCTCC 204033) and S. aureus (ATCC 25723) as experimental strains. Fig. 10 illustrates that the CDs-MoS2 composites show the best antimicrobial effect for both E. coli and S. aureus. Their antimicrobial rate is more than 99 % in both cases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the optimal amount of CDs for the preparation of CDs-MoS2
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the optimal amount of CDs for the preparation of CDs-MoS2 composites ( Fig. 11), in this study, the amount of sulfur and molybdenum sources were fixed based on previous reports related to the preparation of MoS2, and the preparation of CDs-MoS2 composites with different amounts of CDs was carried out. Subsequently, antimicrobial experiments were conducted, and according to the results of antimicrobial experiments, the best antimicrobial effect was achieved when the dosage of CDs reached 80 mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of optimal antimicrobial concentration of CDs-MoS2
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the optimal antibacterial usage of CDs-MoS2 composites against bacteria, gradient antibacterial experiments were conducted herein. Fig. 12 illustrates that there are different optimal antimicrobial usage amounts for the two bacteria, and for E. coli, the antimicrobial rate can reach more than 99 % at 0.0004 g·mL−1. For S. aureus, the antimicrobial rate can reach more than 75 % at 0.0002 g·mL−1; and at 0.0003 g·mL−1, the antimicrobial rate can reach 100 %. Based on the gradient antimicrobial experiments, CDs-MoS2 composites exhibit better antimicrobial effect against S. aureus with less optimal antimicrobial usage.

Glutathione (GSH) is one of the principal antioxidant agents that can scavenge reactive oxygen species (ROS) and shield all ROS-mediated damage. Fig. 13a and b exhibit that group A is the positive control group for the antimicrobial assay, where only the material was added for the antimicrobial assay. Group B is the antimicrobial assay involving the addition of both the material and GSH, and group C is the antimicrobial assay where both the material and GSH were added followed by shock centrifugation to take the supernatant. In group A, no external intervention was done; in group B, GSH was added to exclude ROS-mediated damage; and in group C, only ion release damage existed. The corresponding antimicrobial mechanism is that the difference in antimicrobial rate between group A and group B is attributed to the ROS-mediated damage, the difference in antimicrobial rate between group B and group C is adsorption damage, and the antimicrobial rate in group C represents only ion release damage. According to the experimental results, for E. coli (Fig. 13c), the percentage of adsorption damage in the antimicrobial mechanism was 94.9 %. A very small portion of reactive oxygen damage mechanism occurred and ion damage mechanism was almost absent. However, for S. aureus (Fig. 13d), adsorption damage accounted for 72 %, ROS damage accounted for 27.5 %, and ionic damage was also almost absent. It indicates that for E. coli, the adsorption damage mechanism is the main mechanism, and for S. aureus the adsorption damage mechanism is the main mechanism, and there also exists a certain amount of reactive oxygen damage as a supplement.

The effect of enzyme activities of bacteria can lead to the blockage of metabolic activities of bacteria and make them less viable [45]. The changes in the activity of the relevant enzymes caused by the contact of antimicrobial materials with bacteria can also reflect the damage to the bacteria. Iodine nitrotetrazolium chloride violet (INT), an indicator of cellular respiratory activity, is water-soluble and cleaves at the tetrazolium ring in the presence of respiratory enzymes in living cells, and then it is further reduced to the fat-soluble iodine nitrotetrazolium methyl filth (INF), which gets deposited in the cells [46]. Based on the production of INF, the strength of the metabolic vigor of the cell can be examined. Notably, INF shows a maximum absorbance at 490 nm; therefore, the respiratory chain dehydrogenase activity of the bacteria can be expressed by the absorbance at 490 nm. Fig. 14(a) shows that the respiratory chain dehydrogenase activity of the positive control Control (+) live bacteria was vigorous, and that of the negative control Control (−) dead bacteria was almost completely inactivated. The activity of respiratory chain dehydrogenase of bacteria treated with CDs-MoS2 composites was significantly decreased compared to that of the positive control Control (+). Moreover, the degree of effect on S. aureus was stronger than that on E. coli, which is consistent with the more favorable results of antibacterial experiments on S. aureus.

The ROS produced by antibacterial materials can cause lipid oxidation of bacterial cell membranes, change the permeability of cell membranes, and weaken the metabolic activities of bacteria, thus the degree of lipid peroxidation can also reflect the degree of damage to bacteria. The degree of lipid peroxidation was measured by detecting the content of malondialdehyde (MDA, a polyunsaturated fatty acid oxidation product) in the bacterial broth after CDs-MoS2 treatment [47]. MDA shows the maximum absorbance at 532 nm, thus the degree of lipid peroxidation of the bacterial cell membrane can be expressed by the absorbance at 532 nm. Fig. 14(b) illustrates that the degree of lipid peroxidation of bacteria alone was low in the control group and enhanced in the group of bacteria treated with CDs-MoS2 composites. Moreover, the enhanced lipid peroxidation degree of S. aureus was more significant, which is consistent with the results of the antibacterial experiments on S. aureus. Notably, the antibacterial mechanism is more favorable for S. aureus, and the antibacterial mechanism is complemented by the presence of a portion of ROS-mediated damage to S. aureus. Consequently, CDs-MoS2 composites can lead to a decrease in the metabolic activity of bacterial cells, resulting in an increase in the degree of lipid peroxidation of bacterial cell membranes.

To visualize the external damage of CDs-MoS2 composites on bacteria, SEM was used to observe the morphology of bacteria after their contact with CDs-MoS2. Fig. 15a and b obviously show that the bacteria that were not in contact with the material were intact, full of morphology, and physiologically active. However, the bacteria treated with the material (Fig. 15c, d) underwent severe wrinkling, depression, deformation, or even rupture, leading to the content leakage. The physiological activity was lost and the bacteria died. Compared with S. aureus, the degree of rupture of E. coli was more complete, which is consistent with its antimicrobial mechanism being mainly adsorption damage. S. aureus ruptured to a lesser extent, consistent with the presence of an adsorption damage mechanism and also a part of ROS-mediated damage mechanism. Moreover, the results of the bacterial external damage analysis were also consistent with those of the bacterial internal damage analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial treatment of actual water bodies
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S7 shows a picture of actual Yunnan Dianchi water sampling. Fig. 16 shows a photograph exhibiting the antibacterial effect of CDs-MoS2 on bacteria in Dianchi water samples. Fig. 16(b) shows a pie chart of the number of surviving bacteria in Dianchi water samples, and Fig. 16(c) illustrates a pie chart of the number of living and dead bacteria in Dianchi water samples under CDs-MoS2 treatment. Herein, CDs-MoS2 was used as a wastewater antimicrobial agent for wastewater antimicrobial treatment. It is well known that Yunnan Dianchi water is severely polluted, thus its water was taken to culture bacteria. Fig. 16(a) exhibits a variety of bacterial growth and presence of mold and various other miscellaneous bacteria in the Dianchi water sample. After adding CDs-MoS2, the number of bacterial colonies shown in the figure significantly reduced, indicating that CDs-MoS2 can effectively kill various bacteria in the realistic water environment. Therefore, the CDs-MoS2 composite prepared in this study shows a broad-spectrum antimicrobial effect, which is excellent against E. coli and S. aureus, as well as some molds and other miscellaneous bacteria in the real water body. This presents a potential application in the antimicrobial treatment in water bodies.

In this study, CDs-MoS2 composites with high efficiency and multifunctional response toward polluted water treatment were successfully prepared. The size of CDs-MoS2 composites became smaller than that of MoS2 as observed by SEM. The structural composition analysis showed the successful preparation of CDs-MoS2 composites. The pore size and specific surface area analysis showed that the pore size of CDs-MoS2 composites decreased and specific surface area increased, which was conducive to the enhancement of adsorption properties. The adsorption performance study showed that the CDs-MoS2 composites could effectively adsorb the cationic dye RhB, the anionic dye MG, and the heavy metal ion Cr6+. The adsorption efficiency of CDs-MoS2 composites was 95 % for RhB after 120 min; 98 % for MG after 30 min, which was almost completely adsorbed; and 64 % for Cr6+ after 90 min. The antimicrobial performance study showed that CDs-MoS2 composites exhibited certain broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties. It can effectively fight against Gram-negative E. coli and Gram-positive S. aureus, as well as most of the stray bacteria in the actual water environment. For E. coli, the antimicrobial rate of 0.0004 g·mL−1 could reach more than 99 %. For S. aureus, the antimicrobial rate could reach more than 75 % at 0.0002 g·mL−1 and 100 % at 0.0003 g·mL−1. The study on antibacterial mechanism showed that for E. coli, it was mainly the adsorption damage mechanism, and for S. aureus, it was mainly the adsorption damage mechanism, and a small amount of reactive oxygen damage was also involved. The analysis of bacterial damage showed that the CDs-MoS2 composites were able to break the bacterial cell structure, inhibit the activity of the respiratory chain dehydrogenase of bacteria, and increase the degree of lipid peroxidation of the bacterial cell membrane, which led to the reduction of the metabolic ability of the cells. The adsorption kinetic model fitting analysis showed that the adsorption of RhB, MG, and Cr6+ by CDs-MoS2 composites conformed to the pseudo-second-order kinetic adsorption model. Adsorption isotherm model fitting analysis showed that the adsorption process of CDs-MoS2 composites toward RhB conformed to the (D–R) linear fitting adsorption isotherm model. The adsorption process of CDs-MoS2 composites toward MG conformed to the Langmuir adsorption isotherm model; and that of CDs-MoS2 composites toward Cr6+ conformed to (D–R) nonlinear fitting adsorption isotherm model. The adsorption–diffusion modeling analysis showed that the adsorption process of CDs-MoS2 composites for RhB, MG, and Cr6+ was in accordance with the three-stage diffusion. The excellent properties of CDs-MoS2 composites show a good prospect for multifunctional response toward controlling water pollution.

Wang Qing: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hu Yanling: Resources, Methodology. Yang Zhengfang: Supervision, Resources. Nie Yanli: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Wang Dongquan: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Guo Qianqian: Supervision, Resources. Wen Guanbin: Software, Resources. Wang Qisheng: Supervision, Software. Zhang Bin: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2025.136385.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Adsorption of food dyes from aqueous solution on a sweet potato residue-derived carbonaceous adsorbent: analytical interpretation of adsorption mechanisms via adsorbent characterization and statistical physics modeling[J]
Chem. Eng. J.
